How can Indigenous people and local communities help safeguard nature? "

The researchers reviewed an extensive body of literature to understand how Indigenous peoples and local communities (IPLCs)<sup>1</sup> contribute to caring for nature and solving environmental problems around the world. They found that knowledge and practices of IPLCs are making significant contributions towards addressing current environmental crises
column2: such as climate change and biodiversity loss. Specifically
column3: they identified six ways

column1: 1.IPLCs manage lands through long-standing local practices. For example
column2: in Melangkap village
column3: in Sabah
column6: which sets aside parts of rivers as no-fishing or seasonal-fishing zones. The system helped increase fish populations and later received government recognition.

column1: 2.IPLCs conserve and restore ecosystems. For example
column2: for thousands of years
column3: the Gunditjmara community at Budj Bim in southwest Victoria
column5: built water channels and ponds to farm eels
column6: maintaining aquatic habitats and supporting local biodiversity. The Budj Bim Cultural Landscape is now recognised as a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

column1: 3.IPLCs produce knowledge for assessing and monitoring ecosystems. For example
column2: there are 746 documented climate change indicators developed by IPLCs
column3: reflecting impacts on physical

column1: 4.IPLCs resist resource-extracting industries and defend their lands. For example
column2: the Shipibo-Conibo community in the Peruvian Amazon resisted a palm oil company that illegally deforested 7
column3: 000 hectares of land in their region. They took legal action
column4: filed complaints with international bodies and mobilised global support

column1: 5.IPLCs contribute to global environmental policy development. For example
column2: IPLCs have influenced decision-making on climate change
column3: such as through the Local Communities and Indigenous Peoples Platform within the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change
column4: a formal mechanism that allows them to share knowledge
column5: participate in discussions and help shape global climate policies.

column1: 6.IPLCs offer alternative concepts/philosophies of relations between people and nature. For example
column2: Māori groups in New Zealand/Aotearoa secured the legal recognition of the Whanganui River with rights of personhood
column3: in line with their view of the river as a living entity
column4: which ensured stronger protection to the river.

column1: Six pathways that has been identified in this study.
The knowledge and practices of IPLCs have not been fully recognised in policies for environmental conservation and sustainable development
column2: where scientific and technical approaches continue to dominate. This study presents one of the first comprehensive syntheses of evidence clearly demonstrating the important contributions of IPLCs to addressing today’s environmental crises.

column1: The study suggests that IPLCs and their governance systems should be recognised as key partners and institutions in nature conservation and the sustainable use of natural resources. To ensure justice and equity in nature conservation
column2: it is necessary to respect Indigenous rights
column3: including secure land tenure and free
column4: prior and informed consent. Collaborative approaches
column5: in which IPLCs and scientists co-produce knowledge by combining traditional practices with scientific methods
column6: can improve global understanding of environmental changes and support decisions that protect both people and nature.

column1: Why cancer immunotherapy helps some patients more than others?  "
The researchers examined many scientific studies to better understand how microRNAs
column2: tiny molecules inside cells that control how genes work
column3: affect cancer and the immune system
column4: and what this means for treatment. They found that different microRNAs can have very different effects: some help cancer survive by preventing the immune system from noticing or responding to the tumor
column5: while others make the immune system more alert and active against cancer. They also identified microRNAs that may help explain why some patients can benefit particularly well from immunotherapy – a new treatment that trains the body’s immune system to recognise and attack cancer cells
column6: with fewer side effects than traditional chemotherapy or radiotherapy

column1: Targeting immune checkpoints and the tumor microenvironment
Immunotherapy works for some patients but not for others
column2: and it is unclear why. MicroRNAs can influence both cancer cells and the immune system
column3: so understanding them could help explain these differences. The study is important because it highlights microRNAs’ potential as signals to guide treatment decisions.

column1: In the future
column2: researchers could explore ways to target microRNAs to make immunotherapy more effective against cancer. Doctors could use microRNAs to predict which patients are most likely to benefit from cancer immunotherapy. Patients could eventually receive more personalised and effective treatments with fewer side effects
column3: along with a clearer understanding of how information from tests that measure microRNA levels guides their doctor’s treatment decisions.

column1:
What helps put more women on the scientific conference stage?  "

In recent years
column2: several initiatives have been put forward to increase awareness and reduce implicit biases against women in science. To investigate the effect of these initiatives the researchers of this study analysed the proportions of women among invited speakers at scientific events in evolutionary biology
column3: using data from major conferences along with survey responses from event organisers in the field.

column1: They found that women were underrepresented among invited speakers in specialised courses within evolutionary biology
column2: but roughly proportionally represented in conferences and overrepresented in congress symposia.

column1: These patterns suggest that the increased focus on gender equity through such initiatives may have had a positive effect in some types of events
column2: although disparities persist in others. The researchers also identified several underlying factors that positively influenced the proportion of female invited speakers
column3: such as the proportion of women among event organisers involved in selecting speakers
column4: the organisers’ awareness of gender issues and the presence of visible Equal Opportunity guidelines<sup>1</sup> in event calls.

column1: Women as invited speakers in evolutionary biology events: congress symposia
column2: conferences
column3: and courses.

column1: Although the gender equity (fair opportunities and representation for women and men) at scientific events had been discussed before, earlier works were often based on a small number of conferences or on subjective impressions. This study is important because it provides one of the first systematic, quantitative data-driven analyses of women’s representation across many scientific events, offering robust, large-scale evidence.   The study results suggest that increasing the proportion of women among invited speakers at scientific events will require deliberate actions in the future, such as encouraging women to sit on organising committees, raising organisers’ awareness of gender issues and publishing clear Equal Opportunity guidelines on event websites.    1 For example, guidelines were published on the conference website of The Annual Meeting of the Society for Molecular Biology & Evolution (SMBE) and The European Society for Evolutionary Biology (ESEB). /records/ahvkw-jf377 Gender equity at scientific events Florence Débarre,Nicolas O. Can Lavender and Rosemary help calm nerve cells?  "

The researchers examined how extracts from the plants Lavender and Rosemary affect communication in nerve cells. Nerve cells use electrical signals to send messages to other nerve cells and to the rest of the body. This activity is controlled by receptors and channels on the cell surface including “T-type calcium channels”
column2: which regulate how nerve cells send signals and
column3: through this
column4: influence functions such as sleep and conditions like epilepsy and pain. In laboratory experiments
column5: the researchers used human cells engineered to contain these channels and tested how plant extracts
column6: including essential oils from Lavender and Rosemary
column7: affected them. They found that both types of extracts reduced the activity of theT-type calcium channels
column8:
which lowered the nerve cell’s ability to send signals. This shows that Lavender and Rosemary compounds can act directly on these channels and thereby influence nerve signaling.


Compounds present in Lavender and Rosemary extracts act on T-type calcium channels
column9:
thereby modulating neuronal excitability.

"

Lavender and Rosemary have long been used in traditional medicine and aromatherapy for their calming effects
column10: but it was unclear how they influence nerve cell activity and whether their natural chemicals could be useful in modern medicine against nervous disorders. This study fills this gap by showing that natural chemicals in these plants directly act on T-type calcium channels on the nerve cell surface. By controlling nerve cell activity
column11:
these chemicals could have medical applications. 

The findings provide a basis for further research
column13: to explore how natural chemicals in Lavender and Rosemary could be developed into new therapies for conditions linked to abnormal nerve cell activity
column15: epilepsy or sleep disorders. This is supported by many examples of plant-derived compounds
column18: which have become indispensable in therapeutics. 		/records/m2g3s-gz264	Modulation of T-type Ca2+ channels by Lavender and Rosemary extracts.	Chaymae El Alaoui

column1:
Are UNESCO Biosphere Reserves the missing link to achieving global biodiversity goals? "

The researchers conducted the first global assessment of how UNESCO’s World Network of Biosphere Reserves (WNBR) contributes to nature conservation and the objectives of the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework (KMGBF).

They compared habitat quality inside Biosphere Reserves with that in traditional protected areas (such as national parks and reserves). The study found that Biosphere Reserves generally maintain habitat quality that is as good as
column2: and in some regions even better than
column3: that of protected areas.

column1: The team also assessed how much more land would contribute to the KMGBF’s “30% by 2030”1 goal if Biosphere Reserves were included alongside protected areas. They discovered that global conservation coverage would rise from 16.6% to 19.6% of land. In Latin America and the Caribbean
column2: including Biosphere Reserves
column3: the target would be exceeded by 30%.

column1: The researchers also examined how Biosphere Reserves overlap with key global conservation priorities
column2: such as protecting endangered species
column3: restoring damaged ecosystems
column5: supporting the sustainable use of wild species
column6: storing carbon to reduce climate change and recognising the role of Indigenous peoples. They demonstrated that biosphere reserves could make significant progress across six KMGBF targets.

column1: Finally
column2: they identified 17 regions of the world where expanding the network of Biosphere Reserves after 2025 would best support biodiversity and community needs.

column1:
How Biosphere Reserves Support the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework

" "

The study was conducted because UNESCO Biosphere Reserves are seldom included in the world's assessment of progress towards global biodiversity targets – even though they are one of UNESCO’s primary tools for conservation and sustainable development.
Unlike many protected areas that mainly restrict human activities
column2: Biosphere Reserves aim to combine conservation
column3: local development
column4: education and scientific research. This approach reflects the KMGBF’s focus on harmonising nature with the people living within it.

column1: However
column2: until now
column3: there has been limited global evidence on how effective Biosphere Reserves are or how much they could support countries in meeting international biodiversity targets. This lack of evidence often results in Biosphere Reserves being excluded from national biodiversity plans

column1:
The findings are valuable because they provide scientific justification for recognising Biosphere Reserves as an essential part of global conservation efforts and for aligning the WNBR with the MAB Hangzhou Strategic Action Plan (2026-2035)." "

This research shows that Biosphere Reserves are more than just symbols – they actively protect nature while supporting sustainable livelihoods. The findings suggest that Biosphere Reserves could play a vital role in addressing global biodiversity loss
column2: especially where communities and ecosystems are closely linked.

column1: Recognising Biosphere Reserves in the KMGBF would:
<ul>
<li>accelerate progress towards the 30% by 2030 target
column2: especially in biodiversity-rich tropical countries

column1: <li>support multiple KMGBF targets simultaneously
column2: including restoring nature
column3: protecting genetic diversity
column5: climate mitigation and supporting Indigenous peoples

column1: <li>guide intelligent expansion of the network into regions with the greatest conservation needs and minimal current protection
<li>strengthen cooperation because Biosphere Reserves function as a worldwide network linked through UNESCO.
In brief
column2: the study shows that the World Network of Biosphere Reserves is a strong yet underutilised resource for achieving the world’s biodiversity targets in a manner that benefits both people and the environment. "	<sup>1</sup> Protect 30% of Earth’s land and oceans by 2030.	/records/674w6-rqb11	Potential of the World Network of Biosphere Reserves to advance the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework	Hui Wu
